# MACRO REPORT — 2026-04-22
**Delivered:** 11:44 UTC | **Generated by:** Claude claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Markets are navigating a fragile equilibrium between Iran ceasefire optimism and persistent geopolitical risk, with the US-Iran conflict entering its eighth week but tensions easing after Trump extended the ceasefire indefinitely. The single most important variable for today's session is the Kevin Warsh Fed chair confirmation hearing, which bond markets are treating as the next major catalyst for Treasury yields and rate expectations. Gold continues its powerful bull run, hitting $4,776 as investors seek safe-haven protection amid war-stoked inflation fears, while Brent crude fell sharply by 4.39% on renewed peace hopes. Oil price dynamics—and their feed-through to inflation—remain the binding constraint on central bank easing cycles globally [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-19/kevin-warsh-fed-hearings-will-be-treasury-bonds-next-catalyst].

---

## 1. GLOBAL MACRO OVERVIEW

The dominant macro narrative of 2026 continues to be the US-Israel-Iran conflict and its far-reaching supply-chain and energy price consequences. Global stocks have broadly recovered from peak war-risk levels, with major indices at or near pre-war highs as investors rapidly unwound geopolitical hedges once ceasefire prospects materialised. "The rebound has been driven by a rapid unwind of the war-risk premium that was sitting across equities, oil, and the dollar at the peak of the conflict," noted Billy Leung of Global X ETFs, adding that "positioning that had been defensively tilted for weeks reversed quickly" [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/21/global-stocks-iran-conflict-ai-rally.html]. However, the conflict's shadow persists, with supply chains, shipping routes, and energy prices all remaining under pressure.

US equities ended Tuesday modestly lower, with the S&P 500 declining 0.63% to 7,064.01, the Nasdaq 100 off 0.42% to 26,479.47, and the Dow down 0.59% to 49,149.38, as Iran ceasefire deadline uncertainty weighed on sentiment [Source: Yahoo Finance (^GSPC)]. However, S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 futures climbed after Trump announced an extension of the ceasefire Tuesday evening, with contracts gaining 0.4% and 0.3% respectively [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/live/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-futures-rise-after-trump-extends-us-iran-ceasefire-230429647.html]. Earnings season is providing a significant offset, with 87.5% of the 48 S&P 500 companies that had reported as of last Friday exceeding analyst estimates, far above the long-term average of 67.4% [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-21/us-stock-futures-climb-ai-optimism-earnings-counter-middle-east].

On the monetary policy front, the most critical near-term event is the Senate Banking Committee hearing for Kevin Warsh, Trump's pick to lead the Federal Reserve. Warsh is set to commit to being "strictly independent" on rates, stating that monetary policy decisions must be based on "analytic rigor, meaningful deliberation, and unclouded decision-making" [Source: https://subscriber.politicopro.com/article/2026/04/fed-chair-nominee-warsh-set-to-commit-to-be-strictly-independent-on-rates-00880511]. The 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.26% and the 2-year at 3.72%, below the Fed's current effective rate ceiling of 3.75%, with markets boosting rate cut expectations on the back of oil's retreat from peak conflict levels [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10]. The effective Fed Funds Rate sits at 3.64%, and US CPI came in at 3.32% year-over-year as of March [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL].

The IMF-World Bank spring meetings concluded with frustration from developing-nation policymakers, as repeated external shocks—led by the Iran war's surge in oil and fertilizer costs—continue to derail growth and debt-reduction efforts [Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/battle-scarred-developing-nations-look-path-out-permacrisis-2026-04-20/]. China held its loan prime rate unchanged for an 11th straight month, reflecting a "wait-and-see" posture as policymakers weigh resilient domestic growth against Middle East-driven energy inflation risks [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/20/china-keeps-benchmark-lending-rates-unchanged-as-economic-growth-revs-up-amid-mounting-middle-east-risk-mount-.html]. AI-driven earnings optimism—underscored by Amazon's $25 billion Anthropic investment and J.P. Morgan raising its S&P 500 year-end target—is providing a meaningful counterweight to geopolitical headwinds.

---

## 2. CONFLICT / GEOPOLITICAL STATUS

- The US-Israel-Iran war has entered its eighth week, with the conflict described as an ongoing bilateral confrontation that markets have largely moved to price as "contained." [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/21/global-stocks-iran-conflict-ai-rally.html]
- President Trump announced an indefinite extension of the Iran ceasefire Tuesday evening, lifting S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures by approximately 0.4% after-hours. Diplomatic efforts remain fragile, with Trump acknowledging difficulty dealing with a "seriously fractured" Iranian government. [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/live/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-futures-rise-after-trump-extends-us-iran-ceasefire-230429647.html]
- The Strait of Hormuz situation remains fluid: oil prices surged after Iran reimposed controls, then fell sharply after news of a reopening and renewed peace talks. Brent crude fell 4.39% to $94.16 on the latest ceasefire extension. [Source: https://oilprice.com/Latest-Energy-News/World-News/Long-Haul-Flight-Fares-Soar-as-Iran-War-Hits-European-Aviation-Hard.html]
- The US has been "blockading the blockaders" around Hormuz, with implications extending well beyond the Middle East into global supply chains. China's access to Iranian petroleum is a key diplomatic pressure point Washington is leveraging. [Source: https://www.seatrade-maritime.com/containers/strait-of-hormuz-fears-only-part-of-wider-global-trade-risk-pola-hears]
- War-driven energy costs are feeding through into consumer prices globally: Canada's CPI rose to 2.4% in March (gasoline +21.2% MoM), while the UK's BRC warned that the conflict is "beginning to feed through into UK prices" with headline CPI at 3.3%. [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-20/canadas-annual-cpi-rises-24-iran-war-drives-gasoline-costs]
- Bank Indonesia is expected to hold rates at 4.75% through 2026, with all 31 economists polled by Reuters seeing no cut as Iran war-fuelled energy inflation pressures the rupiah and lifts inflation expectations. [Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/bank-indonesia-hold-rates-475-through-2026-iran-war-fuels-inflation-risks-2026-04-20/]

## 3. FOREIGN EXCHANGE

### Major Pairs

**EUR/USD — ~1.1743** | Bias: Neutral-Bear
- The euro dipped 0.35% against the dollar as the DXY firmed slightly, with ING analysts noting that ECB surveys and stance cap the upside for EUR/USD. The pair is holding above the 1.17 handle but lacks momentum amid Iran ceasefire uncertainty. [Source: Yahoo Finance (EURUSD=X)]
- Key levels: 1.1650 support | 1.1850 resistance
- Catalyst: Kevin Warsh Senate hearing tone on rate independence and Fed path

- Sterling was "little changed" on Wednesday as markets balanced Middle East uncertainty against Trump's ceasefire extension; focus is shifting to upcoming UK inflation data. [Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/sterling-steady-with-focus-inflation-data-iran-ceasefire-uncertainty-2026-04-22/]
- Key levels: 1.3400 support | 1.3600 resistance
- Catalyst: UK CPI data and GBP reaction to Warsh hearing spillover on USD direction

**USD/JPY — ~159.231** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- USD/JPY advanced 0.24% as the yen softened on improved risk sentiment following the ceasefire extension and futures rally. The pair remains elevated with 2-year US yields near 3.72%, limiting the interest rate differential that had previously supported yen. [Source: Yahoo Finance (USDJPY=X)]
- Key levels: 157.50 support | 161.00 resistance
- Catalyst: Warsh hearing implications for US yield direction; BOJ commentary

**AUD/USD — ~0.7157** | Bias: Neutral-Bear
- The Aussie slipped 0.25% as risk-off sentiment from Iran uncertainty weighed, despite a positive copper backdrop. China's decision to hold lending rates unchanged for the 11th straight month limits the growth impulse for commodity-sensitive currencies. [Source: Yahoo Finance (AUDUSD=X)]
- Key levels: 0.7080 support | 0.7250 resistance
- Catalyst: China economic data and Hormuz/oil price developments

**USD/CAD — ~1.3644** | Bias: Neutral
- The loonie steadied near recent five-week highs after Canada's March CPI came in at 2.4%, slightly below the 2.6% consensus, with core measures showing limited pressure outside fuel costs. [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-20/canadian-dollar-hits-five-week-high-mideast-hopes-offset-cpi-data]
- Key levels: 1.3600 support | 1.3750 resistance
- Catalyst: Oil price trajectory and Bank of Canada rate path signals

**USD/CHF — ~0.7785** | Bias: Neutral-Bear
- The Swiss franc remains broadly strong against the USD, with USD/CHF near multi-year lows as the franc benefits from safe-haven demand amid ongoing Middle East conflict. [Source: Yahoo Finance (USDCHF=X)]
- Key levels: 0.7700 support | 0.7900 resistance
- Catalyst: Global risk sentiment; any escalation or breakthrough in Iran diplomacy

- The kiwi edged up 0.06%, showing relative stability amid modest risk appetite improvement. NZD remains anchored by RBNZ caution on rate cuts given elevated global inflation from the energy shock. [Source: Yahoo Finance (NZDUSD=X)]
- Key levels: 0.5850 support | 0.5980 resistance

**EUR/JPY — ~186.936** | Bias: Neutral
- EUR/JPY softened 0.13% as the euro slipped more than the yen, reflecting relative ECB/BOJ policy convergence. The cross remains in a well-established range with Iran risk sentiment as the primary short-term driver. [Source: Yahoo Finance (EURJPY=X)]
- Key levels: 184.50 support | 189.00 resistance
- Catalyst: ECB guidance and Japanese CPI/BOJ signals

**GBP/JPY — ~215.062** | Bias: Neutral-Bull

- Key levels: 212.00 support | 218.00 resistance
- Catalyst: UK inflation data and ceasefire developments

**EUR/GBP — ~0.8709** | Bias: Neutral

- Catalyst: Divergence in UK vs Eurozone CPI data; ECB vs BoE rate path

### Energy

- Brent fell sharply by 4.39% on news of the US-Iran ceasefire extension and renewed Strait of Hormuz diplomacy, pulling back from recent highs near $104. Ceasefire fragility means supply-side risk remains embedded. [Source: Yahoo Finance (BZ=F)]
- Key levels: $90.00 support | $98.00 resistance
- Catalyst: Iran peace talks progress; Strait of Hormuz operational status

**WTI Crude — ~$90.43** | Bias: Neutral-Bear
- WTI declined 1.85%, with Brent-WTI spread widening on Middle East logistics risk. The war has disrupted regional crude flows, and Louisiana Light crude saw extreme volatility in recent sessions per OilPrice data. [Source: Yahoo Finance (CL=F)]
- Key levels: $87.00 support | $94.50 resistance
- Catalyst: US crude inventory data; ceasefire longevity

The market is largely disconnected from Middle East events, with LNG export demand providing modest support. - Copper rose 0.87% to $6.054, with IEA data noting data centres drove half of US power demand growth in 2025, supporting the long-term copper demand outlook. The Iran war's "sulfurous fallout" spreading to copper and nickel markets adds volatility. [Source: Yahoo Finance (HG=F)]
- Key levels: $5.80 support | $6.30 resistance
- Catalyst: China demand data; BHP iron ore output and copper project updates

- BHP reported Q3 iron ore output of 69.8 million metric tons from Western Australia operations while maintaining its full-year outlook, suggesting stable supply [Source: https://www.mining.com/web/laps-of-icy-roads-in-china-show-sodium-batteries-making-an-ev-breakthrough/catl-7/]
- Key levels: Monitor SGX futures for $95/t support | $110/t resistance

- The Nasdaq 100 fell 0.42% but AI optimism is providing a strong floor, with Amazon committing $25 billion to Anthropic and Goldman Sachs noting "consensus earnings estimates have risen consistently in recent weeks." Micron is reportedly "driving over half of the S&P 500's earnings revisions" per Goldman. [Source: Yahoo Finance (^NDX)]

## 8. WEEK AHEAD / SCENARIO ANALYSIS

| SCENARIO | PROBABILITY | TRIGGER | BRENT | S&P 500 | BTC |
|----------|-------------|---------|-------|---------|-----|
| Bull | 30% | Iran peace deal advances materially; Warsh commits credibly to Fed independence; strong megacap earnings beats | $86 | 7,300 | $84,000 |
| Base | 50% | Ceasefire holds tentatively; Warsh hearing inconclusive; earnings broadly solid; oil rangebound | $93 | 7,100 | $79,000 |
| Bear | 20% | Iran ceasefire collapses; Hormuz re-closed; Warsh signals accommodation of Trump rate pressure; oil spikes above $100 | $102 | 6,700 | $71,000 |

### Key Upcoming Catalysts — Week of April 22–26, 2026

1. **April 22 (Today):** Kevin Warsh Senate Banking Committee confirmation hearing — primary driver for Treasury yields, dollar, and rate cut expectations [Source: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/20/us/politics/kevin-warsh-fed-senate-banking-committee-hearing.html]

2. **April 22 (Today/Wednesday):** Boeing Q1 earnings before the open — key indicator for industrial sector and supply chain health; last quarter sales jumped 57% YoY [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/19/earnings-playbook-tsla-ba-lead-a-big-week-of-reports.html]

3. **Week of April 22:** Tesla Q1 earnings call — major AI/EV demand signal; Jefferies raised price target to $350 from $300 ahead of results [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/19/earnings-playbook-tsla-ba-lead-a-big-week-of-reports.html]

4. **April 23 (Thursday):** Intel Q1 earnings after the close — analyst expectations for flat earnings/revenue YoY after prior quarter's 13% stock plunge on soft guidance [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/19/earnings-playbook-tsla-ba-lead-a-big-week-of-reports.html]

5. **Week of April 22:** Bank Indonesia rate decision — all 31 Reuters poll economists expect a hold at 4.75%; outcome will signal broader EM central bank posture on Iran war inflation [Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/bank-indonesia-hold-rates-475-through-2026-iran-war-fuels-inflation-risks-2026-04-20/]

### Disputed

- [Gold has fallen about 8% from its "post-war peak"]: The data bundle states gold fell "about 8% since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran in late February" per CNBC — but the report frames this as falling from a "post-war peak." The CNBC article actually says prices fell 8% *since the strikes began* (i.e., from the pre-war/early-war high), not from a subsequent peak. The framing in the report inverts the directional logic — gold is currently *up* on the day (+1.66%) and the 8% decline refers to a cumulative drawdown from the February strike date to Monday's low, not from some intervening high.
- [Report states the Warsh hearing is "April 22 (Today)"]: The news bundle consistently places the Warsh Senate Banking Committee hearing on **Tuesday, April 21**, not April 22. Multiple sources (Bloomberg, POLITICO, NYT) reference the hearing as scheduled for Tuesday. The report's date is April 22 (Wednesday), making this catalyst listing one day stale.
- [Canada's March CPI consensus was 2.6%]: The KITCO/Reuters article states analysts forecast 2.6% annual inflation and 1.1% monthly — the report correctly notes the 2.6% miss, but also states "core measures continued to show little sign of inflationary pressure outside of the surge in fuel prices" without caveat. The bundle confirms this characterization is accurate per CIBC's Grantham, so this is verified. *(Reclassified — no dispute.)*
- [UNH "faces elevated claims pressure"]: The report adds this editorial characterization but UNH's actual Q1 release showed a raised full-year outlook and only a marginal net margin dip (5.6% vs 5.7% YoY). The bundle's UNH press release contains no mention of elevated claims pressure being a forward concern — this claim is unsupported by cited data.
- [Brent "pulling back from recent highs near $104"]: The mining.com data point shows Brent at $104.4 and crude at $101.85 in what appears to be a recent snapshot, which is consistent. However, the Yahoo Finance bundle shows Brent at $94.16 *as of April 22* with a single-day change of -4.39%, implying the prior close was ~$98.49, not $104. The $104 figure appears to reflect an earlier session price, not the immediate prior close, making the "pulling back from near $104" claim potentially referring to a level from several days prior rather than the prior session.

### Additional Insights

1. **VIX at 19.18 signals complacency risk**: The VIX fell 1.64% to 19.18 — a relatively subdued level given an active war in the Middle East now in its eighth week, a Fed chair confirmation hearing with binary outcomes, and oil still above $90/bbl. This compressed vol environment makes options cheap for event-driven hedges around the Warsh hearing and Boeing earnings today. Traders should note the asymmetric risk: a VIX spike from sub-20 to 25+ is historically fast when catalysts
